title: Quiz: why use background jobs / message queues?
prompt_md: When a user signs up you need to send a welcome email, generate a PDF, and call an analytics API. Why move this work into a background job / message queue instead of doing it all in the request?
options: Because message queues are required by HTTP, so synchronous work isn't allowed
Queues guarantee the email is delivered instantly, faster than doing it inline
Background jobs make the code shorter so there's less to maintain
To keep the HTTP request fast by offloading slow, failure-prone work; the queue makes that work retryable and smooths out load spikes by buffering tasks
explanation_md: Doing slow or flaky work inside the request blocks the user and risks failing the whole response if one step errors. Pushing it to a **message queue / background worker** lets the request return immediately (**low latency**), **decouples** producer from consumer, and makes each task independently **retryable** with **dead-letter queues** for poison messages. The queue also acts as a buffer that **smooths load spikes** — bursts pile up in the queue and workers drain them at a steady rate (**load leveling**). The tradeoff is that the work becomes **asynchronous** and **eventually consistent**, not instant.
